pos\_val\_trad\_df.reset\_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
pos\_val\_trad\_df.fillna(0.0, inplace=True)
print(pos\_val\_trad\_df.to\_string())

Mark V. Pollio, Owner's Expert Report: Please accept this submission as my Report in the arbitration proceeding entitled Columbia Architects v. Warbucks Enterprises, LLC, AAA Case No. 2020-1040. I have been engaged by the attorneys for Respondent-Counterclaimant Warbucks to provide professional consulting and give expert testimony in this proceeding on a single question—did Claimants engage in professional malpractice in failing to incorporate a change in the floor-area ratio in the New York City Zoning Resolution in their architectural plans before construction of the project at issue? My answer to that question is yes: the failure to recognize the change resulted in the design and construction of an illegal extra floor, which has to be removed and has caused immense financial damage to Respondent through delays and extra expenses.
Qualifications
I am an architect licensed in the States of New York, Connecticut, and North Dakota, as well as Italy, France, and the United Kingdom. I am a member of the American Institute of Architects, the Institute for Classical Architecture and Art, the Royal Society of British Architects, the Soane Society, the Society of Architectural Historians, the Italian Society of Architectural Technology (SITdA) and the Académie d’architecture. and hold a certification from the National Council of Architectural Registration Boards (NCARB). I have taught architectural technology at the Universities of Bologna, Padua, Paris 12 Val de Marne, and Imperial College London, and have lectured on aspects of architecture at New York University and the Georgia Institute of Technology. I write frequently on construction topics, including ten books on architecture, which have been issued in many editions and languages and are sometimes used as textbooks in architecture schools that care about architectural history and are not in thrall to the next new thing.
In the 1970s I designed several structures which were favorably reviewed and nominated for important awards. Since then, my commitment to architectural scholarship and writing have left me with little time to compete for commissions. However, I have been frequently retained as an expert consultant and witness. As of this writing I had appeared as a witness at deposition or in trial in 36 court cases and have consulted in over 100 mediations and have given testimony in 73 arbitrations. So frequent have my appearances in these matters been that one lawyer for an adverse party actually called me a professional expert witness! In every case I have been involved in, the tribunal has found me to be a qualified expert. Some tribunals have given more weight to my testimony than others, but all have acknowledged my qualifications.
INFORMATION RELIED UPON
My experience as an expert witness has demonstrated that the credibility and accuracy of my opinions depend greatly on the extent and quality of the background information I have reviewed. To prepare for giving my opinion in this case, I have reviewed all the submissions of the parties to the American Arbitration Association, materials filed with the New York City Department of Buildings and the Board of Standards and Appeals, and relevant provisions of the NYC Building Code, including the Zoning Resolution provisions relevant to the Zoning District in which the structure in question has been erected.
QUESTION PRESENTED AND OPINION
As stated in greater detail below, the building being developed by Respondent Warbucks was intended to be a 20-story mixed-use commercial and residential structure. Claimants’ architectural plans correctly reflected Respondent’s wishes and intent by providing for that height. The zoning regulations permitted this at the time the design work was going through the Schematic Design Phase of the project, but in 2017, while Design Development was being completed, the Zoning Resolution was modified in such a way that the floor-area ratio of the contemplated structure would no longer support a 20-story building. Only after the building had topped out did the building inspectors discern that the structure was a floor too high. A stop work order was issued, and the offending floor had to be removed.
The question I have been asked to give an opinion on is whether Claimant committed professional malpractice in not re-examining the relevant regulatory provisions before issuing their final construction documents and allowing the general contractor to erect the building’s superstructure. Another way of putting the question is whether Claimant’s conduct constituted a departure from the standard of professional care that must guide their work as architects in the State of New York. My opinion is YES — Claimant departed from the standard of care and accordingly committed professional malpractice.
BASIS FOR OPINION
In arriving at my opinion, I assumed the facts stated below to be true. Respondent intended the Building to be 20-stories in height and so advised Claimants. The parties’ relationship is governed by an AIA B101-2007 Owner/Architect Agreement, dated as of May 1, 2016. The following relevant provisions appear in that contract:
§ 3.1.1 The Architect shall manage the Architect’s services, consult with the Owner, research applicable design criteria, attend Project meetings, communicate with members of the Project team and report progress to the Owner.
§ 3.1.5 The Architect shall, at appropriate times, contact the governmental authorities required to approve the Construction Documents and the entities providing utility services to the Project. In designing the Project, the Architect shall respond to applicable design requirements imposed by such governmental authorities and by such entities providing utility services.
These contractual terms required Claimants to “research applicable design criteria,” which included a duty to study the local regulations governing the size and dimensions of the structure they were designing for Respondent’s lot. The resulting drawings had to “respond to applicable design requirements imposed by such governmental authorities.” Thus, the architectural drawings were required to comply with any governmental limitations upon size and dimensions. It was incumbent upon Claimants to “contact the governmental authorities” to be sure they were complying with applicable regulations. The principal such authority here would have been the NYC Department of Buildings (DOB).
As it turns out, the DOB has contended that the height of the building designed by Claimants violates the floor-area ratio (FAR) mandated by Article IX, Chapter 1, § 91-21, of the Zoning Resolution. The FAR provides the formula that allows an architect to determine the allowable height of any building in the City of New York. No violation had occurred at the time Claimant w performing Schematic Design Phase work in 2016, but it appears that in 2017 the NYC Planning Commission recommended, and the City Council approved, a change in the Zoning Resolution that altered the FAR for the Zoning District in which Respondent’s building was being erected.
In 2018, after the superstructure had topped out at 20 stories, the DOB issued a stop-work order based on its contention that the building was too high and that construction could not resume unless and until the structure had been reduced in height by one story. Since then, the project has been in suspense while Respondent has engaged in an appeal process through the DOB and the New York City Board of Standards and Appeals. Two years of delay have set back Respondent’s efforts to complete this development.
Neither the Architect nor Respondent picked up on the change in the FAR. Claimants’ failure to honor the terms of the B101 constitutes a major contributing cause both to the delay and to Respondent being unable to construct a building of the size and height it intended. The failure by the Architect to perform a follow-up review of the Building Code and related regulations constituted a departure from the standard of professional care. Every architect must be aware that building regulations change from time to time and cannot rest content that review of the regulations a year or more earlier will be sufficient at the time construction is about to begin.
I do recognize that the general contractor engaged by Respondent also had a provision in its agreement requiring it to be familiar with and act pursuant to applicable building regulations and that, in its relations with others on the project, such as the Architect of Record, Respondent is to some degree responsible for negligent conduct by the contractors it has engaged. Nevertheless, the Architect runs the project from the standpoint of having responsibility for contract administration (§ 3.6.1) and is supposed to coordinate with Respondent’s consultants. I also recognize that Claimant seem to have some merit in their claim that they relied upon those communications in believing they could proceed using the FAR that was in effect when they began their Schematic Design. None of this, however, diminishes Claimants’ responsibility to be diligent in making a supplemental review of regulations before construction began.
COMPENSATION
My compensation for both consulting services and serving as an expert witness in connection with these proceedings is entirely time-based at a rate of $1,000 per hour. It has also been indicated that Respondent is involved in two other cases relating to the same subject matter, one in state court and the other before the NYC Board of Standards and Appeals, and if my performance in this case results in a favorable outcome for Respondent, it will engage me to serve as an expert in those cases as well.

Demand for energy:

Energy investment:

Investment in green energy:

Depreciation for fossil energy:

Hidden variables for production:

$\eta\_{F,t}$: Shock to hidden variable for production function of fossil energy at time $t$

$\alpha\_{KF}$: Share of capital in fossil energy production function

$E\_{F,t}$: Energy consumption of fossil energy at time $t$

$A\_{G,t}$: Hidden variable for production function of green energy at time $t$

$\eta\_{G,t}$: Shock to hidden variable for production function of green energy at time $t$

$\alpha\_{KG}$: Share of capital in green energy production function

$E\_{G,t}$: Energy consumption of green energy at time $t$

Products and services
We Are All Rare is an organization that offers a wide range of services and solutions for patients, healthcare professionals (HCPs), and life science companies. These services and solutions are designed to improve the quality of life for individuals living with rare diseases and support healthcare professionals in providing the best care for these patients.

Solutions for Patients
Rare Education: Educational and playful programs that aim to improve the lives of children and adults with specific rare diseases, such as Fabry, Pompe, Gaucher, Pompe, MPS I, ASMD, Duchenne, and LAL-D. These programs provide information on disease signs and symptoms, genetic pools, and available treatments. They also offer the patient the knowledge and tools to be more informed and active in their care and decision-making. The program also includes counseling and support for families, as well as workshops and seminars for healthcare professionals (HCPs) to improve their understanding and management of rare diseases.

Rare Navigation: Support program that helps patients navigate local health systems to accelerate access to treatment (if available). Our organization ensures that these services are available and accessible to all patients, regardless of location or socioeconomic status.

Rare Community: Development of community groups for patients with specific rare diseases to foster empathy and support among patients.

Rare Joy: Support activities such as playful events, creativity and creation workshops, paint workshops, and low-impact exercise events (yoga, pilates, stretching, and others) focused on delivering day-to-day life experiences to patients.

Rare Specialized Network: A network of healthcare professionals (HCPs) that support patients navigating their treatment or improvement journey (therapists, physiotherapists, psychologists, geneticists, pediatricians, acupuncturists, nutritionists, and naturists, among others). Our organization's approach to patient-centered care and empowerment ensures that patients are involved in the design and implementation of the program and empowered to take an active role in their care.

Rare Art: Art and photographic events that portray the lives of children/patients that the NGO supports. Our approach to patient-centered care and empowerment is reflected in how we involve patients in creating artwork and selecting photographs.

Rare RWE: Collection of critical data and findings of the NGO's support initiatives to measure effectiveness in life improvement for patients with rare diseases. This program uses various methods such as surveys, interviews, and observational studies to gather data, identify areas of success and areas where further improvement is needed, and make adjustments to the initiatives to ensure they are as effective as possible. The organization may also share this data with other organizations, researchers, and HCPs to contribute to understanding and managing rare diseases. Our approach to patient-centered care and empowerment is reflected in the way we involve patients in the design of the data collection tools and the interpretation of the findings.

Rare Together: Joint ventures with other NGOs and organizations to integrate, expand and scale initiatives in other countries that support patients living with rare diseases.

Rare Tech: Digital tools that support hope, joyfulness, and adherence to treatment and holistic activities. These tools can include telemedicine platforms, patient registries, symptom-tracking apps, and educational resources. The program can also help healthcare professionals (HCPs) and researchers to access and share data more quickly, which can help to advance the understanding of these diseases and improve patient outcomes.

Target Population 
Patients with rare diseases and their families.

Pricing
Rare Education: $50 per patient for a 6-month program.
Rare Navigation: $200 per patient for a 6-month program.
Rare Community: $50 per patient for a 6-month program.
Rare Joy: $50 per patient for a 6-month program.
Rare Specialized Network: $200 per patient for a 6-month program.
Rare Art: $50 per patient for a 6-month program.
Rare RWE: $20,000 per project.
Rare Together: This varies depending on the scope and size of the joint venture project.
Rare Tech: $50 per patient for a 6-month program.

Metrics:
Number of patients educated through the Rare Education program.
Number of patients supported through the Rare Navigation program.
Number of community groups established through the Rare Community program.
Number of support activities organized through the Rare Joy program.
Number of healthcare professionals participating in the Rare Specialized Network.
Number of art and photographic events organized through the Rare Art program.
Number of data and findings collected through the Rare RWE program.
Number of joint ventures established through the Rare Together program.
Number of digital tools developed through the Rare Tech program.

Solutions for HCPs
Rare Education: Educational programs and materials focused on rare diseases, signs and symptoms, genetic pools, and treatment options. These programs are designed to provide HCPs with the knowledge and expertise to understand and manage rare diseases.

Rare Workshop and Seminar: Expert-led rare disease events tailored to specific HCP specialties and diseases. These events provide opportunities for HCPs to learn from specialized sector panelists and Key Opinion Leaders. These workshops will help HCPs to be up-to-date with the latest developments and best practices in their field.

Rare Community: Online communities for HCPs to discuss and learn about real-world evidence information resulting from patient-HCP interactions. These communities allow HCPs to deepen their knowledge about rare diseases and share their experiences with other HCPs.

Rare Tech: Digital tools designed to bring attention, communication, support, interaction, and education closer to HCPs. These tools are focused on a push and pull strategy to develop further awareness about rare diseases and patients’ journeys. These tools may include telemedicine platforms, symptom-tracking apps, and educational resources.
Target Population
Healthcare professionals.

Metrics
Number of HCPs educated through the Rare Education program.
Number of workshops and seminars organized through the Rare Workshop and Seminar program.
Number of HCPs participating in the Rare Community program.
Number of digital tools developed through the Rare Tech program.

Solutions for Life Science Companies:
Rare Funding: Project funding opportunities aim to improve patients' lives with rare diseases. These projects are aligned with the UN sustainable development goals for 2030 and comply with CSR and tax incentives.

Rare Alliance: Development of strategic public and private organizational alliances for rare disease experts. Streamlined projects with multiple stakeholder participation. Joint strategic development of initiatives that help improve patients’ lives.

Rare Insights: Collection of anonymized real-world evidence data in the form of digital real-time metrics dashboards powered by artificial intelligence from developed and ongoing projects. These insights support the improvement of patient diagnostic and treatment journeys for strategic decision and initiative-making.

Target Population
Life Science Companies.

Metrics
Number of funding opportunities provided through the Rare Funding program.
Number of strategic public and private organizational alliances developed through the Rare Alliance program.
Number of real-world evidence data collected through the Rare Insights program.

"Kabbalah Quest - The Tree of Life Journey" is an interactive text-based game that follows the story of Rachel, a spiritual seeker chosen by the Kabbalistic Guardian to save the multiverse from destruction. The game is set in a world inspired by the teachings of Kabbalah, where players must journey through ten different realms, each representing one of the ten sefirot or emanations of the Tree of Life.

The game begins with Rachel receiving a vision of a dark force that threatens to unravel the fabric of reality. She is tasked by the Kabbalistic Guardian to journey through the ten realms, each representing a different sefirah, in order to stop the impending catastrophe.

The first realm is the Keter, or the crown, which represents the highest level of spiritual attainment. This realm is characterized by ethereal clouds, radiant light, and transcendent temples, where Rachel must confront her own ego and overcome obstacles that block her connection to the divine.

The second realm is the Chokhmah, or wisdom, which represents the beginning of creation. This realm is characterized by dark forests, mysterious swamps, and ancient ruins, where Rachel must confront her own intuition and overcome obstacles that block her wisdom.

The third realm is the Binah, or understanding, which represents the feminine aspect of creation. This realm is characterized by misty mountains, hidden caves, and mysterious temples, where Rachel must confront her own voice and overcome obstacles that block her self-expression.

The fourth realm is the Chesed, or kindness, which represents love and compassion. This realm is characterized by serene gardens, tranquil lakes, and radiant temples, where Rachel must confront her own love and overcome obstacles that block her compassion.

The fifth realm is the Gevurah, or strength, which represents willpower and self-esteem. This realm is characterized by fiery deserts, towering volcanoes, and powerful citadels, where Rachel must confront her own ego and overcome obstacles that block her willpower.

The sixth realm is the Tiferet, or beauty, which represents balance and harmony. This realm is characterized by lush jungles, sparkling waterfalls, and sensual temples, where Rachel must confront her own desires and overcome obstacles that block her emotional growth.

The seventh realm is the Netzach, or victory, which represents the masculine aspect of creation. This realm is characterized by dense forests, rugged mountains, and ancient ruins, where Rachel must confront her own primal fears and overcome obstacles that block her path.

The eighth realm is the Hod, or glory, which represents communication and self-expression. This realm is characterized by misty mountains, hidden caves, and mysterious temples, where Rachel must confront her own voice and overcome obstacles that block her self-expression.

The ninth realm is the Yesod, or foundation, which represents the foundation of physical and spiritual existence. This realm is characterized by dense forests, rugged mountains, and ancient ruins, where Rachel must confront her own primal fears and overcome obstacles that block her path.

The tenth realm is the Malkuth, or kingdom, which represents the physical world and the manifest universe. This realm is characterized by diverse landscapes, bustling cities, and grand temples, where Rachel must confront the reality of her actions and the consequences they have on the multiverse.

As Rachel progresses through each realm, she will encounter various allies and enemies, each with their own unique abilities and stories. She will also learn about the interconnectedness of all things and the role that she plays in the grand scheme of the universe. She will discover the truth about her past and her destiny, and will be faced with difficult choices that will determine the fate of the multiverse.

The game will include multiple choices that will affect the outcome of the story, giving players a sense of agency and allowing them to make decisions that will shape their own unique experience. The art style of the game is inspired by traditional Kabbalistic art and the levels are designed to be immersive and engaging, challenging players both physically and mentally, and encouraging them to explore and experiment with different sefirot. The interconnected levels, diverse enemies, and challenging puzzles all combine to create a unique and memorable gaming experience.

"Kabbalah Quest - The Tree of Life Journey" is a unique and innovative take on the interactive text-based game genre, incorporating complex elements of Kabbalah and spiritual teachings in a way that challenges and immerses players in a unique and memorable gaming experience. It emphasizes on the journey of self-discovery, personal growth, and the ultimate goal of attaining spiritual enlightenment.
The player must go throught each one of the 10 realms

All the player choices are remembered and after the game is finished, it asks the player if they want to see a complete psychological and spiritual profile based on the choices made.

# Load the VGG16 model
model = VGG16(weights='imagenet', include\_top=False)

# Function to extract features of an image using the VGG16 model
def extract\_features(image\_path, model):
 image = cv2.imread(image\_path)
 image = cv2.resize(image, (224, 224))
 image = image[...,::-1] # Convert BGR to RGB
 image = np.expand\_dims(image, axis=0)
 image = preprocess\_input(image) # Preprocess the image
 features = model.predict(image)
 features = features.flatten()
 return features

# Function to extract features of all images in the image folder and save them to the features folder
def extract\_all\_features():
 for filename in os.listdir(image\_folder):
 image\_path = os.path.join(image\_folder, filename)
 features\_path = os.path.join(features\_folder, os.path.splitext(filename)[0] + '.npy')
 if not os.path.isfile(features\_path): # Check if features file already exists
 features = extract\_features(image\_path, model)
 np.save(features\_path, features)

# Function to load the features of all images in the features folder
def load\_all\_features():
 features = []
 filenames = []
 for filename in os.listdir(features\_folder):
 if filename.endswith(".npy"):
 features\_path = os.path.join(features\_folder, filename)
 feature = np.load(features\_path)
 features.append(feature)
 filenames.append(os.path.splitext(filename)[0])
 return features

# Function to extract features of the query image, save it to the features folder, and then load the features of all images in the features folder
def extract\_and\_load\_features(image\_path):
 features\_path = os.path.join(features\_folder, os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(image\_path))[0] + '.npy')
 if os.path.isfile(features\_path):
 features = np.load(features\_path)
 else:
 features = extract\_and\_load\_features(os.path.join(image\_folder, file.filename))
 np.save(features\_path, features)
 return features
# Function to perform a search on the features to find the closest matches
def search(query\_features, features):
 knn = NearestNeighbors(n\_neighbors=min(num\_results, len(features)), metric='cosine')
 features = np.array(features)
 features = features.reshape(features.shape[0], -1) # reshape to 2D
 knn.fit(features)
 distances, indices = knn.kneighbors(query\_features.reshape(1, -1))
 # Perform search to find the closest matches
 return indices[0]

@app.route("/", methods=["GET", "POST"])
def index():
 if request.method == "POST":
 # Get the file from the POST request
 file = request.files["file"]
 # Save the file to the image folder
 file.save(os.path.join(image\_folder, file.filename))
 # Extract features of the query image, save it to the features folder, and then load the features of all images in the features folder
 features = extract\_and\_load\_features(os.path.join(image\_folder, file.filename))
 # Perform search to find the closest matches
 results = search(features, load\_all\_features())
 # Display results in a web browser
 return render\_template("results.html", query\_image=file.filename, results=results)
 else:
 # Load the features of all images in the features folder
 features, filenames = load\_all\_features()
 return render\_template("index.html")

if \_\_name\_\_ == "\_\_main\_\_":
 app.run("0.0.0.0")

Abstract
The Eco project is an innovative solution for the voluntary carbon offset market. It utilizes smart contracts and machine learning to create the world's first decentralized autonomous carbon offset registry. The project is built on the Polygon blockchain and allows users to tokenize carbon credits issued by standards bodies such as Verra. These carbon credits are bridged onto the Polygon blockchain and deposited in a Carbon Pool, where they are transformed into fungible tokens called carbon reference tokens. These tokens can be traded and used as a building block for other applications on the Web3 platform.
The unique selling point of the Eco project is its focus on forward selling of carbon reference tokens. This allows for the creation of a new market for carbon offsets and incentivizes all participants to trust the decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) and its token. The DAO governance structure is designed to be efficient and cater to the forward carbon reference tokens market. It includes various stakeholders such as Forest engineers, land owners, carbon offset buyers, and solidity coders. The DAO will also collaborate with companies such as Skylab, which provides precision forestry AI and advanced aerial data analytics for digital forest management.

Read the following passage and wait for the questions that follow: “You might think that borrowing a match upon the street is a simple thing. But any man who has ever tried it will assure you that it is not, and will be prepared to swear to the truth of my experience of the other evening.
I was standing on the corner of the street with a firecracker that I wanted to light. I had no match. I waited till a decent, ordinary-looking man came along. Then I said:
"Excuse me, sir, but could you oblige me with the loan of a match?"
"A match?" he said, "why certainly." Then he unbuttoned his overcoat and put his hand in the pocket of his waistcoat. "I know I have one," he went on, "and I'd almost swear it's in the bottom pocket—or, hold on, though, I guess it may be in the top—just wait till I put these parcels down on the sidewalk."
"Oh, don't trouble," I said, "it's really of no consequence."
"Oh, it's no trouble, I'll have it in a minute; I know there must be one in here somewhere"—he was digging his fingers into his pockets as he spoke—"but you see this isn't the waistcoat I generally…"
I saw that the man was getting excited about it. "Well, never mind," I protested; "if that isn't the waistcoat that you generally—why, it doesn't matter."
"Hold on, now, hold on!" the man said, "I've got one of the cursed things in here somewhere. I guess it must be in with my watch. No, it's not there either. Wait till I try my coat. If that confounded tailor only knew enough to make a pocket so that a man could get at it!"
He was getting pretty well worked up now. He had thrown down his walking-stick and was plunging at his pockets with his teeth set. "It's that cursed young boy of mine," he hissed; "this comes of his fooling in my pockets. By Gad! Perhaps I won't warm him up when I get home. Say, I'll bet that it's in my hip-pocket. You just hold up the tail of my overcoat a second till I…"
"No, no," I protested again, "please don't take all this trouble, it really doesn't matter. I'm sure you needn't take off your overcoat, and oh, pray don't throw away your letters and things in the snow like that, and tear out your pockets by the roots! Please, please don't trample over your overcoat and put your feet through the parcels. I do hate to hear you swearing at your son, with that peculiar whine in your voice. Don't—please don't tear your clothes so savagely."
Suddenly the man gave a grunt of exultation, and drew his hand up from inside the lining of his coat.
"I've got it," he cried. "Here you are!" Then he brought it out under the light.
It was a toothpick.
Yielding to the impulse of the moment I pushed him under the wheels of a trolley-car, and ran.”

"### Your radar for ‘Creepiness’ isn’t that good…

We’ve all likely used the term ‘creepy’ to describe someone or something in our lives at least once. But what constitutes creepy? Is it an underlying intuition? A safe preservation mechanism? Well, Doyle and colleagues (2022) investigated that by employing eye-tracking software to identify precisely where participants were looking the longest when examining thirty faces for attractiveness, trustworthiness, and creepiness. 

They found that the eye region was by far the most looked at and that participants who had previously scored high on ambiguity discomfort were likelier to rate others as ‘creepy’ compared to those who scored highly on fear of the unknown or uncertainty intolerance (yes, these concepts ARE different).

The researchers note that while there is no evidence to support the notion that individuals labelled as ‘creepy’ genuinely pose a threat, this label may cause that individual significant harm. Indeed, our ability to correctly identify people as ‘creepy’ is essential not just for our self-preservation but for preserving others’ from stigma and social marginalisation. 

– by @diana chiang 

### COVID affected the intercultural connections of our hobbies

Naturally following the pandemic, there has been a shift in how we spend our time, who we spend it with, and how much. 

But this also massively differs from culture to culture, country to country. Has COVID made us more locally or globally involved? 

Using network analysis and data gathered from Google Trends based on 50 different countries, Thamprasert, et al. (2021) investigated the interest and country relationships in 10 other hobbies before and after the pandemic. 

The analysis identified that the relationships between hobbies such as art, collecting, and pet care were weak between countries. In contrast, the relationships regarding learning, cooking, music, games, and sports interest became stronger between nations. 

This has exciting applications for widening our understanding of cultural interaction and understanding within a global market.

You can further explore the specific hobby-based relationships between regions in Thamprasert and colleagues’ (2021) conference paper.

– by @diana chiang 

### Emotional well-being impacts our social connections

There have been piles of research suggesting that emotional well-being, feelings of agency, and our social relationships are intertwined, so this might not shock us. However, the directionality of these relationships has long been under question. 

To answer this question scientifically, Vella-Brodrick, Joshanloo, and Slemp (2022) conducted a 13-year-long longitudinal study following the lives of more than 22,000 participants. These people came from various backgrounds across Australia and had an average age of 35 at the start of the study. Survey data were collected four times every four years. 

The results showed that contrary to prior research generally referring to mental well-being as an outcome or consequence of good social relationships and high agency perceptions, mental well-being was a predictor of the two variables. In fact, early social relationship connectedness was not a predictor but a consequence of agency and mental well-being.

These findings pause a long-standing debate and emphasise the importance of taking care of your mental well-being, which will likely yield infinite rewards. 

– by @diana chiang 

---

## You probably underestimate thinking

Inspired by previous studies exploring spontaneous thinking and metamotivation, a collaboration between Aya Hatano, Cansu Ogulmus, Hiroaki Shigemasu, and Kou Murayama set out to discover [what people think about thinking](https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/releases/xge-xge0001255.pdf). 

### \*\*The waiting game\*\*

Throughout five studies across the United Kingdom and Japan, the authors found a cross-cultural, significant effect indicating people underestimate the task enjoyment of waiting. The participants were told to sit in a room without distractions and wait for up to 20 minutes, self-assessing their anticipated enjoyment level (low) before and the actual enjoyment they felt after waiting (much higher). 

### \*\*What’s this mean?\*\*

People rate free thinking as something to avoid, not because it’s inherently unpleasant, but because they don’t appreciate the opportunity for what it could be. This false expectation might be making us unnecessarily avoid thinking in our daily lives and instead reach for distractions, like our ubiquitous phones.

Not necessarily arguing that thinking is inherently fun and enjoyable, the authors think this is the beginning of synergistic research between intrinsic motivation or curiosity and metacognition. 

Who’d a thought?

-by [Lizzie](https://www.notion.so/Lizzie-2d092cfbfa0d45efa90bdeaabde0e40d) 

## It could always be better

This paper is so delightful to read and comes with its own excellent abstract. The authors even preface their work by answering the age old question that yes, it began with an egg. I can’t add an angle (which is ironic in context) to increase Mastroianni and Ludwin-Peery’s paper readability except to provide the spoiler: [our imaginations are biased toward making things better](https://psyarxiv.com/2uxwk/). 

Read on!

-by [Lizzie](https://www.notion.so/Lizzie-2d092cfbfa0d45efa90bdeaabde0e40d) 

---

## \*\*We underestimate our ability to weather emotional storms\*\*

Unsurprisingly, we could be better at predicting our future behaviour. However, what is less well known is that this tendency trickles into our ability to forecast our response to bad news. This ignorance of how we cope can compromise our decisions and undermine our happiness. 

\*\*Why does this happen?\*\*

Well, we often don’t consider how positive experiences might buffer the bad, and we are also more skilled than we realise at construing what happens to us in a positive light. In other words, we have a psychological immune system that shelters us from the worst effects of our misfortune (how exciting!).

\*\*How is this helpful to know?\*\*

Not knowing about this ‘psychological immune system’ can lead us to make decisions that don't maximize our potential for satisfaction. In the words of Daniel Kahneman, rational planning of action becomes extremely difficult if we don't know what we’re going to want and we don’t know how we’re going to feel when we get it. 

Let’s try and learn more about this internal tool we have to weather emotional storms so we can put it to better use!

- by Ella Howes ([https://www.apa.org/monitor/oct01/strength](https://www.apa.org/monitor/oct01/strength))"

CHAPTER TWELVE
 Develop a Habit: Do It Forever, or You’re Cooked
 From Chris
Our great worry with this book—Jeremy’s and mine—is not that the exercises and so on won’t work; they will. It’s you we worry about. Will you take us at our word that the only way to cure back pain forever is to do it yourself . . . to change your own behavior and to do the damned exercises? Maybe, maybe not. We worry that you just won’t get it. Or have the resolve and, well, character to act on it. In this country we are so used to having doctors and other healers do it for us that it is hard to get our arms around this notion. And—as a society—we are a bit idle. We work like crazy at our jobs (maybe too crazy) but not on our bodies or ourselves. Well, if you are going to heal your back pain, you’re going to have to change that. 
The fact is—and we have not come right out and said this before—it makes awfully good sense to do these exercises six days a week. For the rest of your life. That is familiar advice to me. It is exactly what we recommend for the general population in the Younger Next Year books and especially in the excellent Younger Next Year: The Exercise Book, all of which we recommend warmly. But if this is new dogma to you, I would not be stunned to learn that some of you are choking, ever so slightly, on the concept. “Six days a week?” you say to yourself. “Forever, huh?” “Stern,” you mutter to yourself; “very, very stern.” And you do what you’ve done a thousand times before: You make a tiny, private adjustment—somewhere in the back of your mind, where cowardice and self-preservation meet—a private adjustment that says, “That has to be nonsense at some level, a first offer in a negotiation that has to get more sane than that! Here’s what I will do: I will simply do the best I can. Then we’ll see. And don’t forget: Something is always better than nothing.” 
Perfectly sensible. I don’t blame you for one second. All of us make little accommodations with perfection all the time, to keep from blowing our brains out or lapsing into terminal self-hatred. Fine. I have made thousands of such deals myself, maybe a million. And I strongly suspect that they have kept me alive and happy. 
But not this trip. Failure to honor this one is going to make a hash of your life. Jeremy’s injunction is not an opening position; it is the way, the truth, and the light. It really, really is. Ignore this one and you will get messed up fast. You will tumble back into pain in no time and have to start all over again. In agony. And that first sweet flush of recognition and resolution that you got from reading this book will be lost forever. It is like weight loss. If you make a new resolution to lose weight every single morning, you will never, never do it. The first time is not necessarily the last, but it is as easy as it is ever going to get. So, my strong advice on this one is: Make a decision now. Hang on to it like grim death. And never, never let go. It may get more important down the road, as your body goes to hell, but it will never get easier. Now is the time, honest. Don’t be a dope. Do these exercises every day for the rest of your life. You want permanent back pain relief? Study the book and do these exercises forever.
Six Things to Think About
Jeremy is, of course, the expert on all things substantive. But I am a bit of an expert on making excuses to yourself, falling off the wagon, and, you know, generally going to hell. And, oddly, I am also a bit of an expert on how not to do all that. Here is what you need. You need to believe, and I suspect you are pretty far down that road. Then you need to have structure. We’ll talk about that. And you need to have a deeply engrained habit. Here are six things to think about while you are trying to do all that.
First, a touch of good news. Developing a serious exercise habit is one of the easiest of all those things you ought to be doing but are not. Getting a don’t-eat-garbage habit (or dieting) is inexplicably hard and most people fail at it. Giving up the second or third glass of wine? Same thing. But exercise is comparatively easy. Not sure why but one reason is it is doing something, and that is so much easier than promising not to do something . . . denying yourself. Sounds silly but you can just plain make up your mind and do it. Like the Nike slogan: Just Do It! 
Second, there’s this: A good exercise habit feels good. Hard to believe now, maybe, but—once you get so you can do this easily—it is kind of fun. No way to persuade you about that now, except to say “Trust me, it’s true. Been there and I know.”
Third, it’s only ten minutes a day, for heaven’s sake. It will take longer when you’re learning how, but after you get the hang of it, that’s all it will take. Hell, you can hold your breath for ten minutes a day. No, you can’t, but you know what I mean: Ten minutes ain’t long. 
Fourth, it is important to stress that the exercises themselves are not that hard. In the beginning, if you are in pathetic shape, they may be hard. Jeremy tells me that a lot of you simply are not going to be able to do them, as prescribed, for a while. And let’s be realistic: That is apt to be depressing . . . it will feel like a little failure, every morning. Well, tough. You will get there in a couple of weeks or a month, and you know that every day that you try, your feet are on a sacred path: a path away from agonizing back pain and toward a decent life.
Here’s a bit of strictly personal encouragement. I am not the most disciplined guy you ever met. I was never an athlete, and now I am roughly 117 years old. Okay, eighty-three. And I can do them. Do ’em with ease, to tell the truth. So, believe me, you can, too. And once you get there, it is not painful or even unpleasant. In fact—let me repeat this because it is so important—they feel good. It feels good to feel your core tightening beneath you. It feels good to use those mysterious muscles, like your glutes, which are so central to the good life. There is going to be a little surge of pride, most mornings, as you go about it . . . get it right . . . do your body and yourself this enormous favor, with so little effort. 
Fifth, the exercises lend themselves to structure. You mostly don’t have to go someplace (the gym) to do them. You don’t have to struggle to fit them in when you’re traveling. You can set a time and place for them and never have to decide again. You don’t want to have to make a fresh decision about stuff like this every day. Make it automatic, like brushing your teeth. My recommendation: Make it a standard part of your early morning ritual. What I do—and what I bet will work for you—is get up, make coffee, and drink it. Cast an eye over the paper but don’t read it. Personally, I don’t eat breakfast. (And please don’t kid yourself that you need food for strength, or even water for hydration first; that’s simply not true.) Do the damned old exercises and get ’em over with. Then have breakfast, read the paper, walk the dog, or whatever you have to do. 
Sixth and last, make this “your job!” As we’ve said in the original Younger Next Year book, one of the things that most of you (the kind of people who get into our books) have going for you is a work ethic. Some people do not know about that. You do; it’s as natural for you as breathing. You get up every day and go to work. Probably on time. If you have that going for you, it is an inestimable advantage. You do not have to make up your mind to go to work every day, for heaven’s sake; you just do it. Use that work ethic edge. Make these exercises part of your job. And do them every morning as automatically, as mindlessly, as you get up every morning and go to work. Selling on YouTube is easy with Cafe24

After Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok, the e-commerce ecosystem centered around Cafe24 has now expanded its reach to YouTube. The social media powerhouse, with more than 2.24 billion users as of last year, is now open to showcase and sell products from Cafe24 stores on YouTube.

While any cafe24 customer can already market their brand or products online by posting advertisements on YouTube, the new feature is different in that it allows them to provide product information at the bottom of live broadcasts, pre-produced videos, and uploaded videos, along with a separate category called "Store" for viewers who visit their YouTube channel. This provides an environment for more active communication with viewers, who can be called potential customers, centered on products.

It is more significant that Cafe24 is the first Korean e-commerce platform to introduce this feature after Shopify in the North American market. Let's take a closer look at what Cafe24 shop owners can do on their YouTube channel and the expected effects.

YouTube is making e-commerce easier
"We want to make it easier for creators to connect and manage their own stores on YouTube and offer products directly to their audience."

David Katz, VP of YouTube Shopping Products
Until now, it's been difficult for creators with YouTube channels to showcase and sell products they've created, collaborated with, or outsourced from outside companies. Other than adding a link to their shopping cart in the "more" section at the bottom of their video content or in a sticky comment section for announcements, there's been no way to turn viewers into buyers.
Recognizing this, in July, YouTube began to provide more convenient ways for viewers to see what creators are selling when they visit a YouTube channel. For example, while live broadcasting to interact with viewers on their channel, creators can pin photos, prices, and links to their products to the top of the live chat window.

They can similarly pin product information within the viewing screen of uploaded video content to encourage purchases. It's also worth noting that in the store on their YouTube channel, they've organized the various products they carry into a single catalog, so you can see what they have to offer at a glance.

Viewers = potential customers, viewing and purchasing Cafe24 products on the YouTube channel
Cafe24 has made it possible for you to utilize these features without going into the YouTube settings page, and with just a few clicks from the Cafe24 shop admin page. After completing the necessary settings for the integration through the 'Google Channel' app, which provides Google's advertising and marketing features, you can use the YouTube Shopping feature.

The store category created on the YouTube channel can be updated regularly with product photos, pricing information, and product information in the shopping mall to reflect out-of-stock status, just like Facebook Shops, which Cafe24 currently supports. This is also supported for YouTube Live broadcasts and product information pinned to the bottom of the video.

This allows Cafe24 shop owners to utilize YouTube's social media giant to find new customers and communicate their brand and product information in a more engaging way. Another advantage is that there are no fees for live broadcasts using their own online malls instead of external platforms. From next year, these features will also be available in markets such as Japan and Vietnam, where Cafe24 is present.

Image replacement after the service opens
However, YouTube's current policy requires domestic customers to meet additional requirements, such as having more than 1,000 subscribers, 4,000 hours of public video viewing time in the past year, and a Google Merchant Center account that allows you to import store and product information to Google .

At Cafecafe24, we're proud to be a partner to help our creative customers succeed, and it's great to see our efforts translate into collaborations with a global market leader like YouTube.

The Café24-YouTube collaboration will continue in the future to make e-commerce easy and accessible for both YouTubers and shop owners and their subscribers and potential customers. We'll keep you updated on any further developments.

📚References
Ready, Set, Shop on YouTube
Getting started with YouTube Shopping

Grade this essay [The comparison of standards of the cities and small town or villages has been always a debate. Recently, teenagers choose to live in the cities rather than their home villages because of school or job opportunities. This essay will discuss multiple reasons behind this trend and explain why the advantages of being in a city do indeed outweigh its drawbacks.

There are several reasons to desire living in urban areas. Firstly, it gives people an opportunity to study in better schools which cannot be found in rural areas. Since in the modern world education means very much for people’s future, it is crucial to have higher education degrees for those individuals to find well-paid jobs. In addition to that, city life provides people with completely different experiences than their home villages. Thanks to the schools, work or social gathering places, they get to meet a greater number of people from all around the country compared to their rural towns which is crucial for one’s personal development. Lastly, in the cities, not only they get bigger number of job options, but also they can earn larger amount of money. It is very well know that job market is significantly limited in the villages also the current jobs barely pay enough.

It is clearly seen that benefits of leaving villages outweigh its few number of deficits. It is worth to mention that people face some issues, such as being away from their extended family, more competitive and challenging job market, and substantially more expensive living cost, when they move to the cities. Advantages like learning and exploring new experiences, getting a better education leading to a better paid job and having an interesting career, however, surpass the number of the drawbacks of this development.

To conclude, there are various reasons for young generation to leave their homes to live in the cities and this movement’s benefits easily outweigh its disadvantages.

AGE-RELATED
EYE DISEASES
MD UNDERGRADUATE EDUCATION
WEEK 67: DEMENTIA
DR. GRACE QIAO MD FRCSC
PROVIDENCE HEALTH CARE
DEPARTMENT OF OPHTHALMOLOGY 
DISCLOSURES
I have no disclosures.
(These slides are adapted from Dr Stephanie’s Wise delivery
of this lecture from previous years)
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
I respectfully acknowledge that I live and work on the
unceded homelands of the xʷmәθkʷәy̓әm (Musqueam),
Skwxwú7mesh Úxwumixw (Squamish), and sәl̓ilwәtaɁɬ (Tsleil
Waututh) First Nations. 
OBJECTIVES
1. Review ocular anatomy
2. Describe the natural aging changes in the eye including vitreal
changes, refractive error, presbyopia and cataract
3. List and describe the risk factors, signs and symptoms, physician
exam findings, diagnosis and management of the following age-related
eye diseases:
• Cataract
• Posterior vitreal detachment
• Glaucoma
• Age related macular degeneration (ARMD)
4. Recognize the current provincial visual regulations for obtaining and
maintaining drivers licensure in BC
5. List available resources and tools for low vision patients
6. Considerations for the ophthalmic exam in geriatric populations 
ANATOMY 
ANATOMY 
NORMAL FUNDUS
ABNORMAL FUNDUS
Tsui, Jade
1998-03-04 (18), Clinic/Doctor : IRIS Optometry Kitsilano 674
OS Pupil size 4.1mm Field: CENTRAL NASAL Date: 2016-07-20 10:45 AM
Notes:
Tsui, Jade
1998-03-04 (18), Clinic/Doctor : IRIS Optometry Kitsilano 674
OD Pupil size 4.8mm Field: CENTRAL NASAL Date: 2016-07-20 10:45 AM
Notes:
THE ENTIRE FUNDUS
REFRACTIVE ERROR
Main focusing powers of the eye
1. Cornea
• Main focusing power
• Adds 35-45 D of converging power
2. Lens
• Adds 20 D of converging power
• Allows for accommodation (near vision) by changing
shape 
REFRACTION TERMINOLOGY
Emmetropia – “normal”
Myopia – nearsightedness
Hyperopia – farsightedness
Astigmatism – refractive powers in different axes
(think of a football) 
MYOPIA VS HYPEROPIA
Uncorrected myopia Uncorrected hyperopia
Corrected myopia (minus lens) Corrected hyperopia (plus lens) 
Myopia: minus
lenses, minify eyes
Hyperopia: plus lenses,
magnify eyes
PRESBYOPIA
Presbyopia: with aging
there is loss of the ability of
the lens to change shape
(loss of accommodation)
Accommodation: ability of
the ciliary muscle to
contract and the lens to
become more convex 
PRESBYOPIA
• Clinically important in early to mid 40s and
onwards
• Symptoms
• Decreased ability to see near objects
• Distance vision is unchanged
• Treatment: plus lenses 
CATARACT
• Any opacity or discoloration of the
lens – usually reserved for opacities
that affect visual acuity
• Cataracts are described based on
areas in the lens that are affected
• Sub-capsular
• Cortical
• Nuclear
• Can be secondary to:
• Aging – most common
• Congenital or genetic anomaly
• Systemic diseases
• Trauma, radiation, medications
• Age related cataract
• 50% of people 50-75 yrs old
• 70% of people over 75 yrs old 
CATARACT
• Symptoms:
• Gradual onset of decreased vision
• Difficulties in dim or bright light
• Halos and glare
• Monocular diplopia
• Signs
• Decreased visual acuity
• Increase in myopia –”second sight”
• Really means ‘better near vision’
• Due to increase in refractive power of denser lens nucleus
• No pupillary signs (no relative afferent pupillary defect )
• Opacification or discoloration of lens on direct
ophthalmoscopy 
CATARACT
CATARACT
Diagnosis:
• Full eye examination
• Assess for alternate causes
of vision loss
Management:
• Observation – correct
refractive error and follow
• Surgery – cataract
extraction and intraocular
lens implantation
• When symptomatic and
the visual acuity is
<20/50 or resulting in an
impairment in function 
VITREOUS AND AGING
• What is the vitreous?
• Transparent connective tissue,
occupies the space in the
posterior chamber
• Connections to posterior lens,
optic nerve and retina
• With aging there is liquefaction
and collapse of the vitreous
• When this occurs, it can
detach from posterior
structures
• “Posterior vitreal detachment” 
POSTERIOR VITREAL
DETACHMENT
• If it happens gradually and uniformly → symptoms are mild
• If the forces of separation are strong → can lead to retinal tear (in 8-
10%) → untreated, can progress to a retinal detachment
• Symptoms:
• Floaters, flashes
• If there is distorted or decreased vision, there maybe a vitreous
hemorrhage, retinal detachment, or other pathology
• Signs:
• Vision should be preserved, no pupillary findings
• Near translucent vitreal opacity above optic nerve (slit lamp)
• Risk factors
• Increased age – after 60 yrs old
• Myopia (increased axial length of eye)
• Trauma
• Previous cataract surgery
• Inflammatory conditions (e.g. vitritis) 
POSTERIOR VITREAL
DETACHMENT
• Diagnosis
• Dilated fundus
examination to rule out
retinal tear/detachment
• Management
• Counselling
• Non sight threatening (if no retinal tear)
• Symptoms subside in most patients within a few months
• Most patients develop same in fellow eye within 1 year
• Return to clinic for repeat examination if there is a
increase in floaters, flashing lights, decrease in vision or
visual field defect (concern for retinal tear or detachment)
• Follow-up
• Repeat dilated fundus exam within 4-6 weeks or urgently if
there is a change in symptoms 
GLAUCOMA
• The term glaucoma refers to a group of heterogenous
disorders
• All glaucomas have the hallmark of progressive optic
neuropathy and loss of visual field
• This loss of vision is irreversible but often preventable
• Defining feature of glaucoma = glaucomatous optic
neuropathy
• Characterized by damage to the optic nerve (cupping)
• Visual field defects resulting from progressive optic nerve
damage
• Initially often peripheral (asymptomatic)
• Central vision is often preserved until late in disease
course (too late… screening is crucial!)
GLAUCOMA
Is classified based on the morphology of the drainage angle
• Open
• Closed
• What is open or closed?
GLAUCOMA
OPEN ANGLE GLAUCOMA
• Insidious onset
• Risk factors: ↑IOP, ↑age, African or
Hispanic descent, family history,
thinner corneal thickness, myopia,
trauma, steroid use
• Symptoms: painless, slow
progressive peripheral vision loss
• Signs:
• +/- Relative afferent pupillary
defect
• +/- Elevated intraocular
pressure (IOP)
• Constricted visual fields
• Optic nerve appearance
(cupping, hemorrhage,
notching)
CLOSED ANGLE GLAUCOMA
• Sudden onset
• Risk factors: Inuit or East Asian
descent, hyperopia, ↑age, female sex,
family history
• Symptoms: pain, nausea, decreased
vision, halos
• Signs:
• Very elevated IOP
• Fixed mid-dilated pupil
• Significantly reduced visual acuity
• Red and injected conjunctiva
• Tearing
• Shallow anterior chamber
• Hazy cornea
GLAUCOMA
GLAUCOMA
What is the CUP? 
GLAUCOMA 
GLAUCOMA
• A word on intraocular pressure (IOP)
• Elevated IOP is one of the most important risk factors for
open angle glaucoma
• Elevated IOP alone is not sufficient to make a diagnosis
• Diagnosis
• Open angle glaucoma – depends on the optic nerve and
visual field appearance
• Closed angle glaucoma – depends on examination
findings of a closed angle, increased IOP and resulting
optic neuropathy 
GLAUCOMA
• Serial visual field tests
reveal progressive loss
(left eye - blind spot is
temporal)
GLAUCOMA
Management: despite the many risk factors
in glaucoma, the only modifiable risk factor is
IOP
Medical Therapy
• Topical IOP lowering agents
• Example: latanoprost
• Prostaglandin analogue
• MOA: works by increasing spaces
between muscle fascicles in ciliary
body, thereby increasing uveoscleral
outflow
Laser Therapy
• Laser trabeculoplasty
• Laser peripheral iridotomy
Surgical Therapy
• Fistulizing procedures
• Microinvasive procedures (stents)
ARMD
• Age-related macular degeneration
• Incurable, bilateral, acquired
degenerative disease of the macula
• Leading cause of irreversible central
visual loss (<20/200) among people
>age 50
• Leads to central vision disturbances
• Can lead to inability to drive, difficulty
recognizing faces and significant
challenges with ADLs
• 2 forms: dry and wet
ARMD – DRY
Most common form – 90% of cases
• Slowly progressive
• Less significant visual impairment
• Characterized by drusen (granular lipid rich material) and
atrophic pigmentary changes
ARMD – WET
Presence of choroidal neovascular
membrane
• Rapidly progressive
• Leads to significant central visual loss
• Characterized by macular hemorrhage and macular
edema
ARMD
• Risk factors
• Age (>55)
• Female sex
• Light coloured iris
• Family history
• Smoking, hypertension
• Symptoms
• Many early dry ARMD patients remain asymptomatic
• Blurred vision, gradual or sudden
• Distortion or metamorphopsia
• Central or paracentral scotoma (“blind spots”)
• Signs
• Decreased visual acuity
• Amsler grid test findings
• Drusen, pigmentary changes or choroidal neovascular membrane in
macula 
ARMD
Metamorphopsia Central scotoma
 Amsler Grid
ARMD
• Diagnosis
• Macular examination
• Optical Coherence Tomography scanning
• IV fluorescein angiogram (leakage of dye from vessels)
ARMD
Management
• Dry:
• Smoking cessation, improve dietary intake of
green leafy vegetables, manage hypertension, UV
protection
• AREDS2 vitamin supplementation
• Vit C, Vit E, zinc, copper, lutein, zeaxanthin
• Decreases rate of progression from intermediate
ARMD to advanced ARMD
• Low vision aids
• Regular Amsler grid testing to detect acute vision
changes → urgent review with ophthalmologist
• Wet:
• Above, plus…
• Intraocular injections of anti-vascular endothelial
growth factors (anti-VEGF)
• Vision stabilizes and often improves as leakage
from abnormal vessels is stopped 
DRIVING VISION STANDARDS
Visual Acuity
>20/50 with both eyes open & examined together
• Commercial: 20/40 or better
• Emergency: 20/30 or better
• 20/60 or 20/70 - RoadSafetyBC may request an on-road
assessment
• <20/70 - usually won't request on-road assessment
• Restriction: corrective lenses required
DRIVING VISION STANDARDS
Visual Field
• 120’ continuous horizontally, and 15’ continuous above and below
horizontal meridian
• Commercial: same
• Emergency: 150' horizontal, 20' above & below fixation
DRIVING VISION STANDARDS
A Physician’s Role
• Assess measures of visual function
• Consider other medical conditions that may be additive to vision
impairment:
• Substance use, dementia, sleep disorders, neurologic disorders or
dysfunction (seizures, stroke), brain injury, endocrine/ metabolic disorders,
MSK disability
Reporting
• Section 230 of the Motor Vehicle Act: report to RoadSafetyBC any patient
who has a medical condition that makes it dangerous for them to drive AND
continues to drive after you’ve warned them not to
• Physician is protected from any legal action brought for making a report
• Physician can be found liable for failing to report in provinces with mandatory
requirements if a patient of theirs causes damage or injury
LOW VISION
Patients with vision loss from any ocular disease may require
vision rehabilitation
• WHO?
• Any patient with vision impairment that is not treatable
(refer after evaluation by ophthalmologist)
• WHAT?
• Low Vision Clinic at VGH Eye Care Centre
• Canadian National Institute for the Blind (CNIB)
• Introduction and demonstration of lighting, contrast and aid
devices
• Mobility and orientation training 
LOW VISION 
OPHTHALMIC EXAM IN
GERIATRIC PATIENTS
Barriers to care
• Communication
• Accessibility/transport
• Cognitive function
• Diagnosis
• Treatment
• Follow up
OPHTHALMIC EXAM IN
GERIATRIC PATIENTS
Office accessibility
• Parking, elevators
• Walking distance
Waiting room and exam room considerations
• Mobility limitations
• Walker/wheelchair accommodations
Equipment
• Near visual acuity testing
• Hand held slit lamp
• Limited testing due to positional limitations
Patient support person
BE FLEXIBLE, DO YOUR BEST
OPHTHALMIC EXAM IN
GERIATRIC PATIENTS
May be unable to measures VA
• Rely on observation of activities, functioning, ADLs
May need accommodations for treatments/surgery
WE COVERED A LOT OF GROUND!
• Anatomy
• Refractive error
• Presbyopia
• Posterior vitreal detachment
• Cataract
• Glaucoma
• Age-related macular degeneration
• Driving standards
• Low vision
• Ophthalmic exam in geriatric
patients

S:
Thrusts and priorities of the Coalition 
The most important common denominator of this government is the weakening of liberal institutions and the strengthening of national and religious collective principles. What this can mean in its more extreme form could be observed during the coalition negotiations: There were demands for the unlimited detention of asylum seekers, for a right for doctors to refuse to treat certain groups (e.g. from the LGBTQI community), or for the removal of the provision that racism is one of the reasons for which one can be disqualified for election to parliament. The Israeli government is thus following a path also taken by other movements or governments committed to majoritarian principles, such as in Poland, Hungary, the United States, Brazil, and India. Yet, the anti-liberal impetus in Israel has specific implications, because it is not only directed per se against liberal values or against what is classically understood as a danger to ethnonational collectives (for example, immigration). In Israel, there is also the conflict with the Palestinians, which has not only a territorial but also an identity-determining dimension for the state. The criticism of the universality of human and minority rights is sometimes combined in the discourse on the Palestinians with an openly displayed disregard for international law. The transformation of the Judiciary 

Looking at the plans of the new coalition, there is hardly an area in which the government is not seeking changes in the normative foundations and functioning of institutions. The intent is to transform the management of the occupation of the West Bank, education, the position of women, the LGBTQI community, as well as the Arab minority, the judiciary, the police, the relationship between state and religion, and citizenship law, among other issues. This government is being driven by an antiliberal, cultural revolutionary momentum.

As a rule, two thrusts emerge: First, the functional imperatives and checks and balances by which the institutions operate are to be undermined in favour of a political majoritarian principle; second, ethnonational principles are to guide action in the aforementioned areas over the long term. This approach becomes directly visible, for example, when Ben-Gvir is given exclusive powers to prescribe behavioural guidelines for the police, when internal military powers are assigned directly to the new ministry under Smotrich, or certainly most conspicuously in the planned curtailment of the rights of the judiciary and the Supreme Court. The latter is of such great importance because the Supreme Court is the only institution that exercises an effective control function vis-à-vis parliament. This applies in particular to its judicial review extrapolated from the Basic Law “Human Dignity and Liberty”. The Israeli system of government has no counterweights or limits on power in the form of a second chamber, no presidential prerogatives, no comprehensive constitution, and no federal structure.
The Court has used judicial review since 1995 to reject 22 laws and other government decisions, to the great annoyance of the right, who criticise this practice as being fundamentally undemocratic. By comparison, the German Federal Constitutional Court has at least partially overturned or required amendments to more than 200 laws in the same period. Justice Minister Levin has now presented a comprehensive plan for how to weaken the Court. For example, the election of judges is to be decided by a governmental majority in the future. In addition, the legal principle of reasonableness is not to be applied to government decisions. Most important, however, is the “overrule clause”.

This would allow parliament to overrule judicial review procedures, that is, rulings of the Supreme Court based on Israel’s Basic Laws with a simple majority – this would cease to apply only if 12 out of 15 judges supported a repeal of the law. Ultimately, this reform would undermine the already limited principles of liberal democracy in Israel. For without judicial review, the parliamentary majority hardly has any other limitations. Ultimately, the legal basis on which the Court operates can also be changed. At the same time, the judicial reform creates the preconditions for further reforms that have so far failed at the Court or were not even initiated. Some politicians have already announced that they want to re-pass laws that have already been rejected by the Court in this legislative period without any changes: These include the legalisation of formerly illegal outposts in the West Bank, the introduction of indefinite security detention, and the exemption of Haredim members from military service. Executive decisions that the Court had also annulled could also be reinstated in the future, such as the routine disqualification of various Arab parties before Knesset elections or the public funding of gender-segregated events. Last but not least, the outcome of the indictment against Netanyahu is likely to depend on the (remaining) strength of the judicial Apparatus. The occupied territories 

There is a consensus on the right that only the Jewish people have a historical claim to the West Bank, that Israel will no longer withdraw from it, and that there cannot be a Palestinian state between the Jordan River and the Mediterranean Sea. The Coalition Framework Agreement already states in the first sentence: “The Jewish people have an exclusive and inalienable right to all parts of the Land of Israel [...] – Galilee, Negev, the Golan, and Judea and Samaria.” Therefore, this government is far from looking for a compromise with the Palestinians. Rather, the aim is to decide the conflict as unilaterally as possible and to perpetuate control over large parts of the Palestinian territory, especially Area C. All members of this government also support at least partial annexations (see numerous interviews on this in the journal Ribonut), but they have different opinions as to whether this is just strategically prudent – Likud appears to deny this. However, the coalition agreement with Religious Zionism (§118) states that the prime minister is to design a policy for the transfer of “sovereignty” (i.e. annexation) to the West Bank. Exactly what this should look like is still unclear. Likud politician Levin formulates what strategy the coalition should follow if annexation is not officially carried out: He says the government must try to “hold the maximum amount of territory and apply sovereignty over the maximum amount of territory while keeping the Arab population within it to a minimum”. Levin thus describes a process that can be called de facto annexation, namely the legal integration of settlements and settlers into the Israeli legal system, although occupation law prevails in the West Bank, and thus the commanderin-chief of the relevant military unit is also the sovereign under international law. The Israeli think tank INSS concludes from the coalition agreements that Israel is in the process of accelerated annexation. In fact, Likud has already announced that the new government will implement a reform for the “civic equality of settlers”, but without changing the legal status of the territories. Parts of the coalition have also stressed that they want to change the status quo on the Temple Mount – a measure that has a particular potential for escalation. In general, it is feared that violence in the West Bank will continue to increase. Already under the government of Naftali Bennett and Yair Lapid in 2021/22, violent clashes between the Israeli military, the Palestinians, and the settlers have reached an all-time high since the end of the Second Intifada. Among parts of the new government, it seems at least questionable whether they are interested in calming the situation or seek escalation in order to justify further action against the Palestinians. Above all, the fact that Ben-Gvir and Smotrich have far-reaching administrative as well as police powers in their newly tailored ministries contributes to this fear. This means that two key positions with a decisive influence on the living conditions of Palestinians in the West Bank are now in the hands of people who want to resettle the Palestinian population.
